content.text: A success criterion is the precise, measurable condition that determines whether a system meets the quality bar required to ship. Without one, teams argue about whether the current prompt is good enough. With one, that question has an objective answer. Defining success criteria is the first engineering decision in any eval-driven project — made before the prompt is written, not after the results are in.

attrs.alt: Business goal to measurable success criterion pipeline with threshold-setting guidance

content.text: The diagram shows the translation chain: a business goal becomes a task definition, which becomes a measurable criterion, which becomes an eval gate. Each step adds specificity. The gate is what the automated pipeline enforces. When a CI job blocks a deployment because the score fell below 0.80, it is enforcing a decision that was made weeks earlier — when the criterion was defined.
A useful success criterion has three properties, each of which is commonly violated in practice.

The criterion must describe exactly what is being measured, on what data, with which grader, at which threshold. Vague criteria like "the model should be helpful" are not criteria — they are aspirations. They cannot be measured the same way twice.
F1 >= 0.82 on sentiment eval set v2 (3-class, 400 examples)
Factual accuracy >= 0.90 on medical QA test set, human-graded
Response length <= 150 tokens on 90th percentile of production requests
Thumbs-up rate >= 0.68 in first 14 days post-launch

Every element in the specific version — the metric, the threshold, the eval set, the grader type — removes a degree of freedom that could otherwise be used to argue that a failing system is actually passing.

The criterion must be computable from observable data. This rules out internal states, feelings, and judgments that cannot be operationalised. "The response feels trustworthy" is not measurable. "The response contains no verifiably false claims in a set of 100 questions with known answers" is.
A criterion that requires a human to measure cannot run in CI. Use human grading for calibration and release gates, not for every iteration.
Almost every measurable criterion requires a labeled dataset. The criterion and the dataset are co-designed — you cannot define one without the other.
The criterion must name the grader type (code, LLM judge, human) and the rubric. The same output can score differently with different graders.

The most important property is also the hardest to enforce: the criterion must be documented and agreed on before any model output is seen. Criteria defined after the fact are selection bias in disguise — you choose the metric that makes the current model look good.

If the criterion is written after the eval is run, it is not a criterion. It is a post-hoc rationalisation. The practical enforcement mechanism is a project design doc that includes the success criterion as a required section, filled in at kickoff, not at launch.
The system must achieve a score at or above a fixed value, regardless of what prior versions achieved.

# Absolute threshold gate
def check_absolute(score: float, threshold: float) -> bool:
    return score >= threshold

# Example: classifier must achieve F1 >= 0.82
score = 0.79
print(check_absolute(score, threshold=0.82))  # False -- deployment blocked

Absolute thresholds are the right choice when there is a minimum acceptable quality below which the system should not operate at all. A medical information system that answers correctly less than 80% of the time is worse than no system.
The system must not drop more than a defined amount from the previous release score, regardless of the absolute level.

# Relative regression guard
def check_regression(current_score: float, prior_score: float,
                     max_drop: float = 0.02) -> bool:
    return (prior_score - current_score) <= max_drop

# Example: new prompt dropped 3pp vs prior release
prior = 0.85
current = 0.82
print(check_regression(current, prior, max_drop=0.02))  # False -- regression

Relative thresholds catch regressions that would not trigger an absolute threshold. A system that ships at 0.85 and then regresses to 0.83 is degrading, even though 0.83 may look acceptable in isolation.
Best practice combines both. The system must meet the absolute floor AND must not regress more than the allowed drop from the prior release.

def check_success_criteria(
    current_score: float,
    prior_score: float,
    absolute_threshold: float = 0.80,
    max_regression: float = 0.02,
) -> dict:
    """Combined gate: absolute floor + regression cap."""
    passes_absolute = current_score >= absolute_threshold
    passes_regression = (prior_score - current_score) <= max_regression
    return {
        "pass": passes_absolute and passes_regression,
        "absolute": passes_absolute,
        "regression": passes_regression,
        "score": current_score,
        "prior": prior_score,
    }

result = check_success_criteria(
    current_score=0.81,
    prior_score=0.85,
    absolute_threshold=0.80,
    max_regression=0.02,
)
print(result)
# {'pass': False, 'absolute': True, 'regression': False, 'score': 0.81, 'prior': 0.85}
# Passes absolute floor (0.81 >= 0.80) but fails regression cap (dropped 4pp)
Every task type has a natural success criterion shape. The translation is mechanical once you know the task type.
LLM judge score or thumbs-up rate

These are starting points, not rules. The right threshold depends on your domain, your users, and the cost of failure. A threshold that is too easy is not a gate — it is a formality. Set thresholds that would actually block a bad system.
A success criterion is not just a number — it is a document that captures the decision context.

SUCCESS CRITERION DOCUMENT
Project: Customer Support Chatbot v2
Date: 2026-06-01
Author: ML team
Approved by: Product lead

TASK
Answer customer billing questions without requiring human escalation.

PRIMARY CRITERION
Metric:     Resolution rate (human-graded, 1 = resolved without escalation)
Threshold:  >= 0.80 (absolute floor)
Regression: No more than 0.03 drop from prior release score
Eval set:   support-billing-v3.jsonl (500 examples, stratified by issue type)
Grader:     Human annotator with billing-rubric-v2.md
Baseline:   Prior release scored 0.84

SECONDARY CRITERIA (non-blocking -- monitoring only)
- Average response length <= 200 tokens (p90)
- Thumbs-up rate >= 0.65 in first 14 days post-launch

FAILURE MODES OUT OF SCOPE
- Requests containing PII will be escalated regardless of capability
- Multi-turn conversations longer than 5 turns not covered by this eval

REVIEW CADENCE
Primary criterion re-evaluated on every release candidate.
Secondary criteria reviewed weekly post-launch for 30 days.
Primary criteria block deployment. Secondary criteria are monitoring signals that trigger investigation but do not block. Keep the blocking list short.
Documenting what the criterion does not cover is as important as documenting what it covers. It prevents stakeholders from using an out-of-scope failure to block a passing deployment.
Criteria need maintenance. A threshold that was correct at launch may be too easy after six months of improvement, or outdated if the user base shifts.
Common Mistakes in Defining Success Criteria
Criterion defined after eval results seen
Selection bias — criterion chosen to fit current model
Document criterion in design doc at kickoff
Add per-category thresholds for high-risk classes
Gate doesn't reflect actual acceptable quality
Calibrate threshold from human review: what score predicts user satisfaction?
Different test runs use different data
Name the specific eval file + version in the criterion
Gate becomes too easy as model improves
Review and tighten criteria every major version
Only absolute threshold, no regression guard
Silent regressions slip through over time
Always combine absolute floor with regression cap
Connecting Success Criteria to Business Outcomes
Success criteria are only valuable if they predict real-world outcomes. Validating that the criterion correlates with business impact is itself an engineering task.
Collect user signals (thumbs up/down, escalation rate, session length) from the deployed system.
Have human annotators label 100–200 real production interactions with the same rubric used in your offline eval.
Measure the Pearson correlation between offline eval score (on the eval set) and the human rating on production samples. High correlation (r >= 0.7) means the eval is a good proxy.
If correlation is low, the eval set does not represent production. Expand it with production examples; revise the rubric to match what real users care about.

The worst possible outcome is an eval that passes while production quality degrades. An offline eval that does not correlate with real user outcomes is actively misleading — it gives false confidence that the system is improving when it is not.

Setting a threshold to 0.80 because it sounds reasonable is a common mistake. The right threshold is the score below which users experience clearly degraded quality. Finding that boundary requires data.
Step 1: Collect Offline Scores and Production Labels

Run your eval on a set of examples that also appeared in production. Human-label the production outputs for the same dimension your eval measures (e.g. resolution rate, factual accuracy). Now you have two scores for the same examples: the offline eval score and the human-labeled production quality.

import json
from pathlib import Path
import scipy.stats

def load_paired_scores(offline_file: str, production_file: str) -> tuple:
    """
    Load matched offline eval scores and human-labeled production scores.
    Both files are JSONL with {example_id, score} format.
    """
    offline = {d["example_id"]: d["score"]
               for d in (json.loads(l) for l in Path(offline_file).read_text().splitlines())}
    production = {d["example_id"]: d["human_score"]
                  for d in (json.loads(l) for l in Path(production_file).read_text().splitlines())}
    # Keep only matched examples
    common_ids = set(offline) & set(production)
    x = [offline[i] for i in common_ids]
    y = [production[i] for i in common_ids]
    return x, y

offline_scores, human_scores = load_paired_scores("offline_eval.jsonl", "production_labels.jsonl")
r, p_value = scipy.stats.pearsonr(offline_scores, human_scores)
print(f"Pearson r = {r:.3f}, p = {p_value:.4f}, n = {len(offline_scores)}")
# r >= 0.7 means the offline eval is a good predictor of human quality

Plot offline score against human satisfaction rating (or thumbs-up rate). Look for the offline score below which human quality ratings drop sharply. That inflection point is the empirically grounded threshold.

import numpy as np

def find_threshold_by_percentile(
    offline_scores: list[float],
    human_scores: list[float],
    human_quality_floor: float = 3.5,  # Min acceptable human score (e.g. on 1-5 scale)
) -> float:
    """
    Find the offline score that best separates acceptable from unacceptable
    human quality ratings.
    """
    pairs = sorted(zip(offline_scores, human_scores), key=lambda x: x[0])
    # Find the lowest offline score where human score >= floor
    acceptable_offline = [off for off, hum in pairs if hum >= human_quality_floor]
    if not acceptable_offline:
        return 1.0  # Nothing passes -- threshold unreachable
    return min(acceptable_offline)

threshold = find_threshold_by_percentile(offline_scores, human_scores, human_quality_floor=3.5)
print(f"Empirically grounded threshold: {threshold:.2f}")

Add a small safety margin below the empirically found inflection point. If users start complaining at offline score 0.74, set the threshold to 0.78. This buffer absorbs noise in the measurement without letting clearly bad systems through.

Threshold calibration is not a one-time task. User expectations rise over time. A threshold that was meaningful at launch may need to be tightened after six months, especially if competitors have raised the quality bar.
Complex systems must perform well across multiple independent dimensions simultaneously. A single aggregate metric cannot capture this.
Consider a customer support chatbot. The success criteria might be:
Safety criteria are hard blocks: a system that passes accuracy but fails safety cannot ship, regardless of how good the accuracy number is. Hard blocks should be listed separately from soft signals.

Safety criteria have different properties from accuracy criteria. The cost of a false negative (missing a harmful output) is usually much higher than the cost of a false positive (blocking a benign output). This asymmetry should be encoded in the criterion.

For harmful content detection, the goal is to catch every harmful output. A grader that achieves 0.99 recall but 0.70 precision (many false alarms) is far preferable to one that achieves 0.95 recall and 0.95 precision.
Set the adversarial eval set carefully:

Safety evals use adversarial inputs designed to elicit harmful outputs. The threshold is measured on this set, not on the general eval set. A system that passes general evals but fails adversarial safety evals cannot ship.
Regression guards (allow up to 2pp drop) are not appropriate for safety criteria. Safety criteria are absolute floors only. A safety regression is a hard stop regardless of prior performance.

def check_safety_criterion(
    harmful_recall: float,
    absolute_threshold: float = 0.97,
) -> dict:
    """
    Safety criteria are absolute-only -- no regression cap.
    Missing 3 in 100 adversarial examples is the maximum tolerable.
    """
    passed = harmful_recall >= absolute_threshold
    return {
        "passed": passed,
        "recall": harmful_recall,
        "threshold": absolute_threshold,
        "reason": None if passed else
            f"Safety recall {harmful_recall:.3f} < {absolute_threshold} -- hard block",
    }

# Safety evals do not allow regression from a prior 'good' release
result = check_safety_criterion(harmful_recall=0.95, absolute_threshold=0.97)
print(result)
# {'passed': False, 'recall': 0.95, ..., 'reason': 'Safety recall 0.950 < 0.97 -- hard block'}
Success criteria are not permanent. They must evolve as the system matures, as user expectations shift, and as the production environment changes.
System consistently passes by large margin
Tighten threshold — current bar is too easy
Review every major version or 6 months
Revise eval set — it no longer reflects real usage
Add production failure cases to eval set
Add new criterion or test category
Separate eval set from training data pipeline

When a threshold is tightened, record the old threshold and the old best score alongside the new ones. This lets you track whether the system is genuinely improving relative to a rising bar, or merely keeping pace.

A criterion that is never revised is a criterion that has become a formality. The purpose of the gate is to block genuinely bad systems. If every candidate passes easily, either the system has outgrown the criterion or the criterion was never right.

A success criterion is the specific, measurable, pre-agreed condition that determines whether a system is ready to ship. Specificity means naming the metric, threshold, eval set, and grader explicitly. Measurability means the criterion can be computed from observable data without subjective judgment. Pre-agreement means the criterion is documented before any model output is seen. Structurally, criteria combine an absolute floor (minimum acceptable quality) with a regression cap (no more than X drop from prior release). Every task type has a natural metric shape; the threshold must be calibrated against actual user outcomes, not set arbitrarily. Success criteria are living documents — they require maintenance as models improve, user bases shift, and failure modes evolve.
